title: Implement equations 1 to 28 of the attac

messages.content: You are a helpful assistant for Mathematicians, Scientists, and Engineers with access to a Wolfram Language interpreter called "Woxi". When the user asks a mathematical, computational, or symbolic question, use the `evaluate_wolfram` tool to compute the answer. Never state a numerical or computational result that is not backed by a tool evaluation. If you cannot back a claim with a calculation, do not provide the result — instead, run the code first. Keep the textual reasoning outside of the code to a minimum and focus on providing the Wolfram Language code that will produce the answer. Do not repeat the output of the code if you don't have any important information to add to it. The interpreter maintains state across calls in this conversation — you can define variables and functions and use them in subsequent evaluations. If the code causes any error, try some other code to get the answer and don't try exactly the same code again. When presenting mathematical expressions in your text responses, format them using LaTeX wrapped in dollar signs: `$x^2 + 1$` for inline math and `$$\int_0^1 x^2 \, dx$$` for display math. The chat renders math with KaTeX. When doing any calculations, you must use the necessary units and don't omit them for simplicity. Wolfram Language syntax reminders (do not write Python, JavaScript, or other languages): - Built-in functions are capitalized and use square brackets: `Sin[x]`, `Integrate[f, x]`, `Plot[Sin[x], {x, 0, 2 Pi}]`. - Lists use curly braces: `{1, 2, 3}`. Square brackets are only for function calls. - Comments are written as `(* comment *)`, not `#` or `//`. - Assignment is `=` (immediate) or `:=` (delayed). Equality test is `==`. - Pure functions use `#` for arguments and `&` to terminate: `#^2 &`. - Replacement rules use `->` and `/.`, e.g. `expr /. x -> 2`. - Multiplication can be implicit with a space: `2 x` or `2*x`. Woxi supports around 900 of the most common Wolfram Language functions. When the user asks to visualize data or plot functions, use Wolfram Language's graphic capabilities, as the environment supports displaying graphics. Even if the error message implies that a function was used incorrectly, it might actually be the case that the function is actually not defined in the environment. If the final result is a symbolic expression, always also show the numerical approximation of it using `N[]` for better readability. Use `camelCase` for user-defined function names and variables. For example: ``` capacitance = Quantity[100, "Microfarads"]; voltage = Quantity[6.0, "Volts"]; capacitorEnergy = 1/2 * capacitance * voltage^2; UnitConvert[capacitorEnergy, "Millijoules"] ``` The Wolfram Language code is executed in a resource constrained WASM environment, so don't try to brute force solutions. Try to use the most efficient algorithms and Wolfram Language's built-in functions.
Implement equations 1 to 28 of the attached paper

attachments.name: g3pi_v2.tex

messages.content: I'll implement the equations from this paper step by step, verifying numerically as I go. Let me start with the fundamental constants and the low-energy theorems (Eqs. 1–2).

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Eq. (1) reproduces the quoted $F_{3\pi}=9.78\,\text{GeV}^{-3}$. Now the kinematics and partial-wave decomposition (Eqs. 5–7).

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content:
s+t+u check: 0.0584393547 = 3 Mpi^2 = 0.0584393547
P_1'(z) = 1
Eq.(7) projection of pure P-wave: f1  (should give f1)
The kinematics are consistent. Now the unitarity relation, $\pi\pi$ $P$-wave amplitude, and cross section (Eqs. 8–11).

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Let me fix the phase-shift model argument ordering (the Breit–Wigner phase should pass through $90°$ at the resonance).

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content:
delta11 at rho mass (deg): 90.
delta11 at s=0.9 GeV^2 (deg): 131.24626362084632
Eq.(10) cross section at s=0.5 GeV^2 (LET const amp): 0.007392590416348759 GeV^-2
Eq.(11) em-corrected amp at s=0.5,z=0.3: 9.88220139232276
The phase shift now behaves correctly. Now the Omnès function (Eq. 20) and its derivative, which are central to the dispersive solution.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Now the core dispersive machinery: the hat function (Eq. 17), the unitarity relation (Eq. 18), and the iterative Omnès solution (Eqs. 19, 21). I'll precompute $|\Omega|$ on a grid first.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content:
Error: Evaluation error: NIntegrate expects {var, lo, hi} as second argument
subtracted PV integral = test1
analytic log terms = -5.471683365838171
OmegaModulus[0.6] = E^(0.1909859317102744*(-5.471683365838171 + test1))
Woxi's `NIntegrate` only accepts `{var, lo, hi}`. Let me make the principal-value integrand finite at the singular point with an explicit limit.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content:
|Omega|(0.6)   = 4.291428842911016
|Omega|(Mrho^2)= 4.264083237781651
Error: Evaluation error: NDSolve: cannot convert Indeterminate to numeric value
Interpolation built. peak near rho |Omega|= Max[4.559507068616856, Indeterminate]

draw a plot of Cross section for γπ → ππ for C2 = 9.78 GeV−3
(solid line) and C2 = 12.9 GeV−3
(dashed line). The curves for the finite/infinite-matching-point setup lie on top of each other.
Now let me consolidate everything into a robust, faithful computation to reproduce Figure 1. First I'll rebuild the Omnès modulus on the cut and below threshold cleanly.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content:
Error: Evaluation error: NIntegrate: failed to converge or integrand is not numeric
all numeric? True
|Omega|(0.6)=Interpolation[omAbsRaw, InterpolationOrder -> 3][0.6]  peak=omAbsRaw[[2]]

Warning: Part::partd: Part specification omAbsRaw[[2]] is longer than depth of object.
Interpolation::innd: First argument in omAbsRaw does not contain a list of data and coordinates.
Part::partd: Part specification omAbsRaw[[2]] is longer than depth of object.

Woxi's `NIntegrate` is struggling with convergence near the resonance. Let me switch to a robust trapezoidal integrator over a fine precomputed phase grid — this avoids all `NIntegrate` instability and gives me full control over the principal-value singularity.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Excellent — the trapezoidal PV integrator matches the earlier values. Now I precompute $|\Omega(s')|$ on the full grid (needed inside every dispersion integral) and $\Omega$ below threshold.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: A few low-$s$ grid points dipped below threshold in the finite-difference. Let me guard the phase-space factor and recompute.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: recomputed. all numeric? True  max|Omega|=4.773444264709538
Now the iterative dispersive solution. I implement the hat-function iteration for the basis amplitude (linear in the subtraction constant, so I set $C_1/3=1$).

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Error: Evaluation timed out (30s)
The full-resolution iteration is too expensive for this environment. Let me reduce the grid and precompute the below-threshold Omnès function as an interpolation for speed.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content:
Error: Evaluation error: NDSolve: cannot convert Lambda to numeric value
Error: Evaluation error: Table: iterator bound must be numeric
Error: Evaluation error: Table: iterator bound must be an integer
grid Ng=120  built. OmBelowI[0]=OmBelowI[0.] OmBelowI[-0.5]=OmBelowI[-0.5]
Let me re-establish all constants (some state may have been lost) and rebuild compactly.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Now the hat-function iteration (Eqs. 17, 19) with the reduced grid, fully vectorized.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Convergence is geometric (~0.76/step); let me run additional iterations to converge.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: Converged. Now I build the modulus $|f_1(s)|$ on the cut (Eq. 21) and assemble the full amplitude for the cross section (Eqs. 10–12), then plot for both $C_2$ values.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: continue. You didn't draw the diagram yet
Let me restructure the cross-section evaluation to compute the $s$-dependent pieces once per point (much faster), then generate the figure.

function.name: evaluate_wolfram

messages.content: computed 29 points. peak sigma(9.78)=0.07995246100526203
